destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Had a great time learning about the Moche culture with Diego our guide. We bought tickets on the spot at the ticket area, including the museum and actual archeological site. We would recommend visiting the archeological site first to get a better undestanding of the history of Huaca de Luna. The site offers free guides in Spanish and English so make sure to ask the ticket office for their timings. We joined a Saturday 10am English guide. During our tour, Diego was great at explaining the history of Moche and the significance of each site we visited. It was a very engaging tour and our experience was definitely enhanced thanks to him! As we had a very thorough explanation of Huaca during our tour, I felt we were able to appreciate the different artefacts more so compared to vice versa.
label: positivo

text_clean: Sacsayhuamán is genuinely impressive. The sheer size of the stones and the fact that these structures have lasted this long make you stop and think about the level of planning, labour, and motivation it must have taken to build them. One of the more impactful sites in the area, especially if you’re choosing carefully which ruins to visit. Martin Boyce Barbados

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is the only continuously occupied Incan town, it is very beautiful with cobblestoned streets and adobe buildings and streams neatly directed through it by Incan stonework and a nice market of highland wares for your perusal. Visible and drawing your attention on the surrounding hillsides are a number of huge granaries/storehouses where the Incas stored the agricultural surplus. Ollantaytambo sits in the picturesque sacred valley on the course of the Urubamba River and is guarded on the abutting hillside by the formidable ruins of Incan terraces and religious structures. These you can tour with a ticket, for which the sacred valley multisite-card also serves. This requires significant stair climbing of varying heights but is well worth the views near and far that can only be had from there. The stonework is amazing and the details up top simply cannot be fully experienced from below or from pictures. As always, I cannot emphasize enough how worthwhile it to hire a guide to expound on what you are seeing. There is so much subtle detail and context thay can easily be missed, consider how miniscule amount of money it costs to hire a guide compared to the overall cost of taking a trip to such a remote but amazing place.

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.
label: positivo

text_clean: Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. Thanks 😘

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is the only continuously occupied Incan town, it is very beautiful with cobblestoned streets and adobe buildings and streams neatly directed through it by Incan stonework and a nice market of highland wares for your perusal. Visible and drawing your attention on the surrounding hillsides are a number of huge granaries/storehouses where the Incas stored the agricultural surplus. Ollantaytambo sits in the picturesque sacred valley on the course of the Urubamba River and is guarded on the abutting hillside by the formidable ruins of Incan terraces and religious structures. These you can tour with a ticket, for which the sacred valley multisite-card also serves. This requires significant stair climbing of varying heights but is well worth the views near and far that can only be had from there. The stonework is amazing and the details up top simply cannot be fully experienced from below or from pictures. As always, I cannot emphasize enough how worthwhile it to hire a guide to expound on what you are seeing. There is so much subtle detail and context thay can easily be missed, consider how miniscule amount of money it costs to hire a guide compared to the overall cost of taking a trip to such a remote but amazing place.

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.

There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.

Lamentablemente mi sobrina piso algo punzante de metal q gracias a Dios no se lo clavó. Fuí a hacerle saber a los guardias y me explicaron q todos los dias limpian las playas pero q la gente no colabora eso es muy triste. Me dijeron q vaya al museo q alli me darian unos polos por colaborar con la reserva, los chicos entusiasmados quisieron ir pero lamentablemente ya no tenian polos y les regalaron un librito para pintar y desarrollar unos laberintos, les dieron también esas sintas donde se cuela el alcohol, mascarillas y un toma todo de metal muy lindo. Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is the only continuously occupied Incan town, it is very beautiful with cobblestoned streets and adobe buildings and streams neatly directed through it by Incan stonework and a nice market of highland wares for your perusal. Visible and drawing your attention on the surrounding hillsides are a number of huge granaries/storehouses where the Incas stored the agricultural surplus. Ollantaytambo sits in the picturesque sacred valley on the course of the Urubamba River and is guarded on the abutting hillside by the formidable ruins of Incan terraces and religious structures. These you can tour with a ticket, for which the sacred valley multisite-card also serves. This requires significant stair climbing of varying heights but is well worth the views near and far that can only be had from there. The stonework is amazing and the details up top simply cannot be fully experienced from below or from pictures. As always, I cannot emphasize enough how worthwhile it to hire a guide to expound on what you are seeing. There is so much subtle detail and context thay can easily be missed, consider how miniscule amount of money it costs to hire a guide compared to the overall cost of taking a trip to such a remote but amazing place.
label: positivo

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.
label: neutro

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.
label: neutro

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is the only continuously occupied Incan town, it is very beautiful with cobblestoned streets and adobe buildings and streams neatly directed through it by Incan stonework and a nice market of highland wares for your perusal. Visible and drawing your attention on the surrounding hillsides are a number of huge granaries/storehouses where the Incas stored the agricultural surplus. Ollantaytambo sits in the picturesque sacred valley on the course of the Urubamba River and is guarded on the abutting hillside by the formidable ruins of Incan terraces and religious structures. These you can tour with a ticket, for which the sacred valley multisite-card also serves. This requires significant stair climbing of varying heights but is well worth the views near and far that can only be had from there. The stonework is amazing and the details up top simply cannot be fully experienced from below or from pictures. As always, I cannot emphasize enough how worthwhile it to hire a guide to expound on what you are seeing. There is so much subtle detail and context thay can easily be missed, consider how miniscule amount of money it costs to hire a guide compared to the overall cost of taking a trip to such a remote but amazing place.

text_clean: Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. Asistí con unos sobrinos argentinos de 12 y 9 años, al explicarles lo q era una reserva comenzaron a recolectar vasitos, envolturas y más. Lamentablemente mi sobrina piso algo punzante de metal q gracias a Dios no se lo clavó. Fuí a hacerle saber a los guardias y me explicaron q todos los dias limpian las playas pero q la gente no colabora eso es muy triste. Me dijeron q vaya al museo q alli me darian unos polos por colaborar con la reserva, los chicos entusiasmados quisieron ir pero lamentablemente ya no tenian polos y les regalaron un librito para pintar y desarrollar unos laberintos, les dieron también esas sintas donde se cuela el alcohol, mascarillas y un toma todo de metal muy lindo. Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.

Por tanto un escenario precioso con estas graves falencias de seguridad. Espero que no haya accidentes que lamentar

Lamentablemente mi sobrina piso algo punzante de metal q gracias a Dios no se lo clavó. Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Lo que menos nos gustó de nuestro viaje a Perú. Las líneas en sí son interesantes, pero todo lo que rodea a la gestión deja mucho que desear. Es muy caro para lo que ves, más el pago de 2 tasas diferentes una vez allí. Además, el vuelo en la avioneta dura media hora y las explicaciones son prácticamente inexistentes más allá de "aquí tenemos la figura del mono" En esa línea. Si pillara más de paso... Bueno. Pero pilla muy a desmano para solo media hora de sobrevuelo y ninguna explicación. Nos explicaron mil veces más cosas en Paracas sobre la única figura del candelabro que se encuentra allí, que en todas estas figuras en Nazca.

text_clean: If you choose to take the 1-day-ticket it includes four inka places that are located along the 28G-road north of Cusco. Sacsayhuaman is the main one. Take a bus from the main road to go to the attraction on top first (Tambomachay). From there walk down the road to see the other ones and finish with Sacsayhuaman. Do not take a taxi, they pretend to be very helpful but in the end only want to make money. You should have a kind of passion for old inka places. Otherwise 70 PES / 18 € are not worth the entrance fee.

text_clean: Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

text_clean: There's an entry gate at the start of the climb with no signs indicating this is a paid activity. The ticket office is right at the top and entry price is 70 soles ($20) per person. Clearly relying on a sunk cost approach of unprepared people doing this climb at high altitude and not wanting it to be for nothing. The ruins look great and impressive but dishonest price gouging is simply ugly. We decided not to enter and walk around instead. That's 80% of the experience right there. Charge less or be clear at the entry to the climb that there is a charge so that people can make an informed decision beforehand.

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.
label: neutro

We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

El servicio de guiado es gratis, sin embargo se recomienda hacer una colaboración voluntaria, ya que por pandemia estuvieron sin trabajo y el único ingreso que tienen los guías a mi parecer son las colaboraciones. 4. Al finalizar el recorrido hay una mini feria de artesanía, el precio es aceptable. Si llevas varias cosas te obsequian algo pequeño. No recomiendo regatear precios ya que son artesanos y viven de su trabajo. 5. Antes de comenzar el tour hay una cafetería, los precios algo altos pero te salvan de cualquier antojo. 6. La entrada se compra adelante, justo donde se encuentra el museo. Lo recomendable es comenzar el recorrido en el museo y luego ir por la huaca. 7. Recorrido aproximado de 1 hora. 8. El museo y la huaca cuentan con estacionamiento gratuito. A veces no hay guardián pero no se ve peligroso.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Es difícil puntuar como corresponde al Museo porque hay varios factores para tener en cuenta. Para empezar, cuando una entra al estacionamiento, tiene que volver a la entrada para adquirir el ingreso y el valor nos pareció más bien de índole simbólica * 10 soles para adultes * 4 soles para adultes mayores y estudiantes *1.50 soles niñes. Ingresamos en subida al mismo, y me quedé pensando, si la subida es tan empinada, ¿Cómo hacen para ingresar las personas con movilidad reducida o usuaries de silla de ruedas? Adentro del museo, el calor, hacia mitad del recorrido se hace realmente muy pesado, sumándole a la experiencia el uso obligatorio que tenemos que hacer como corresponde de la mascarilla. La exposición entera, está muy bien armada, sólo a veces (muy pocas en realidad) el relato construido para guiar la visita cambia de persona, al principio se mantiene con una "objetividad" y luego pasa a una primera persona colectiva. A tener en cuenta algunos de los termómetros no funcionan (: Es la cuarta vez que viajo hasta Lambayeque para poder compartir esta belleza de museo, en esta ocasión, fui acompañada de tres personas más y resulta que la última sala que es realmente la frutilla en el postre. Casi que el impulso principal para ver tan increíble sala viva, resulta que está inhabilitada, cerrada por covid. Lo cual, es ilógico debido a que estamos dentro del museo y esa sala, no se diferencia en exposición del resto de las salas. Así que, sigue siendo recomendable, pero deberían reactivarla.

text_clean: Unbelievable in person. Pictures do not do it justice. Here’s some helpful tips as an American tourist on their first trip to Macchu Picchu: 1 Buy your tickets well in advance. There is a huge line at the tourist office in Aguas Calientes for last minute ticket purchases. It’s chaotic, skip the headache and plan ahead. We were out in Aguas Calientes for two nights and both nights the line / crowd was huge. Do your research on the best time to go. We went early October (last week) - the temperature was perfect. Crispy in the early morning then warmed up later on. Sun is strong, wear sunscreen and bug spray. 2 If you can, and are up for the challenge, do the hiking trail up. Not the Inca Trail. There’s a walking path that’s about ~1+ hour (longer if you take breaks). It’s 100% worth the effort for the views. It’s like the stair-master on hard. There are buses that run very frequently up but they’re not for the faint of heart or for impatient people who hate waiting in long lines. The buses fly up the sides of the mountain (no thank you). 3 Get an early ticket. It gets crowded. We had a 9am and it was great. It was crowded when we were leaving about 2 hours later. 4 There’s a snack counter and a formal restaurant. The snack counter is 10/10 with beer, cocktails, desserts, and snacks (chicken empanada is A1). Grab something before you go in as you can’t eat when you’re in there. 5 You cannot get in ahead of your ticket time. They’re strict about this. 6 BRING YOUR PASSPORT 7 Get a guide. There’s no informational signs that describe what you’re looking at. If you don’t have someone describing things you’re just there for the views (even though we left with more questions than answers) 8 Bring soles for the bathroom. 2 soles to enter. 9 Respect the beauty of the site
label: neutro

text_clean: Mi familia y yo visitamos el Monasterio de Santa Catalina y quedamos impresionados por su belleza. Este lugar es ideal para tomar fotos profesionales, ya que los ambientes son simplemente maravillosos y la arquitectura es impresionante. El costo de entrada para adultos es de 40 soles, pero es genial que los niños menores de 2 años y los adultos mayores de 65 años no tengan que pagar entrada. Dentro del monasterio, tienes la opción de contratar un guía por un costo adicional de 20 soles, pero nosotros optamos por explorar por nuestra cuenta para ir a nuestro propio ritmo. El lugar cuenta con diferentes ambientes, baños convenientemente ubicados y tachos de basura en todas partes para mantenerlo limpio. El paisaje es verdaderamente hermoso, con numerosos jardines y fuentes que añaden un toque de serenidad al lugar. Definitivamente, es un destino que recomendaría para una visita en familia.

Las agencias cobran el tour 90 soles el cual incluye el bus desde Chachapoyas los pasajes en teleférico, almuerzo, y guía. Se recomienda visitar el lugar aparte de ser impresionante se puede apreciar varios micro climas.

Lo único malo y no es por parte de los agentes turísticos es el problema del tren mi agente en todo momento me decía que ni bien baje del bus de retorno inmediatamente reservar tu boleto del tren si no están pendientes uds. mismo pueden perder el tren y quedarse un día en el pueblo y el hostal mas barato esta 120 soles y la comida ni que decir, el tren algunas veces llega a llevar solo 400 o 500 pasajeros y eso si sacas con tiempo tu ticket de retorno solo hay 3 salidas del tren 2 5 y 9 de la noche, en ese dia que viaje era el numero 160 y mi retorno estaba programado a las 5:00pm y solo dijeron que del 30 al 200 subirán al tren y el resto turno de las 9:00pm de la noche ese mismo día a promediar 9:50pm cae una fuerte lluvia con crecida de rio y hubo derrumbe en esa zona hubo 3 desaparecidos y las demás personas quedaron varados todo esto me entere en la mañana con mi agente de turismo pues el decía que si abordaría en el tren y otras personas que estaban con ella mucha no llegaron en ese día....pero como dice lo vivido nadie me lo quita nada mas hermoso de conocer dicha maravilla👍

text_clean: The square is located in Lima old town and it is a must see if you're visiting Lima. The oldest building in the square is the cathedral, which is now not used for messes. The second most important building is the presidential palace, very nice indeed and with a guard change show held every day. The other buildings are from in colonial style, in the typical ocra colour. The square is nice during the day, however it becomes a bit dangerous at night, so make sure you visit it with daylight.

text_clean: Lugar increíble, caminata nivel 2. Debes hidratarte pero si te ven agua te la quitan, llévala en tu mochila. Por ningún motivo dejes basura, restos de alimentos ni te lleves piedras. text_clean: Ancient sacred city in the clouds! The 600 hundred year old architecture and stone work is amazing! Walk on one of the ancient Inca trails and you will come across the famous Inca Bridge. To get the best experience, I recommend a tour guide from a reputable company to appreciate the knowledge and history of Machu Picchu. Be prepared to wait in long cues.You are not alone on the mountain. There are thousands of tourists pounding the walk ways. And sometimes it is a slow walk as the narrow paths are blocked by people taking selfies or that iconic photo. There are no toilets or bathrooms on the site. These are at the gate entrance only. Time is also limited, you can go either to the morning or afternoon session. Feel safe, as there are security discreetly watching. Some areas are restricted with no touch or no entry signs, due to tourists having silly accidents, and also others causing damage to structures. You will see archaeologists at work and maintenance people too. The mountain is accessible to people who have some mobility. Even people with walking sticks and elderly were able to experience the mountain. Remember to take your passport to get the famous Machu Picchu stamp. I would love to go to Machu Picchu again one day.
label: neutro

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.
label: neutro

text_clean: Hermoso lugar, lleno de una gran y especial energía. Nosotros contratamos una agencia, lo que facilitó todo el viaje. Nos pasaron a buscar en bus al hotel, nos entregaron nuestros boletos del tren y del bus para subir a Machu Picchu. Incluso pudimos dejar las mochilas con un encargado que las llevó a nuestro hotel de Aguas Calientes. Así subimos livianos, solo con lo necesario. Tuvimos una guía, Eli de agencia Pacifico, que nos dio una charla maravillosa de cómo vivieron en esa linda ciudad. Y a mediodía nos dejó libres para que recorramos a nuestras anchas. A las 2 almorzamos y todo ok. Lo único es la espera para poder bajar. La fila para tomar los buses llegaba hasta la curva del camino, pero es solo paciencia. Es un viaje 100% recomendado.

text_clean: Machu Picchu ⛰ La forma más barata de visitar Machu Picchu es teniendo tiempo: ⛰ De Cusco tomar un transfer hasta Ollantaytambo temprano en la mañana y el trayecto dura 1 hora con 30 minutos. La agencia te recoge en el hospedaje. ⛰ Luego otro viaje en tren local y como es tan solicitado por los propios Peruanos, toca hacer la reserva con tiempo para poder encontrar cupos; la prioridad es para los locales. ⛰ Para subir a las ruinas, el paquete incluye la entrada pero si llegas por ti mismo; existen dos formas: la paga y la gratis. Puedes usar el bus que vale para extranjeros 12 dólares por trayecto. Aceptan efectivo y tarjeta. El viaje tarda 25 minutos. ⛰ La forma gratis es subir toda la montaña en un sendero señalizado y la subida dura casi tres horas. La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.
label: neutro

text_clean: Beautiful place! Unfortunately we bought tickets for Circuit 1B which felt like a scam. You walk in the jungle for most part of it, then You just get to see 1 ancient building from afar then take the famous photo and that’s it. People from management won’t let you even watch other circuits from afar. All in all it’s like looking at this marvel through a fence. Avoid the circuit for sure. Aside from that prepare yourself for long lines to get into the bus. Is they say “be there at 10”, be there at 9 or 9:30 at most. The place at the gates is well kept and clean, you can have snacks and use toilets for 2 soles.

text_clean: Fue la razón de mi viaje a Perú. Subí dos veces en diferentes dias. Un dia compartiendo el guía y la siguiente vez con guía privado. Gran diferencia. El lugar por si mismo vale el viaje, las incomodidad de los horarios para tomar el avión a Cusco. Ya que no hay vuelos directos de México hacia allá. Por fuerza tienes que hacer escala en Lima. Vale la pena después de haber pagado un boleto de tren tan caro de Oyataitambo hacia Aguascalientes. Haber pagado una noche de habitación en ese lugar. Todo eso valió la pena. La subida en el bus hacia el lugar del Machupicchu simplemente fascinante. Las vistas desde dentro de los vestigios son maravillosas. El pensar como construyeron toda esa ciudadela, es algo que te deja asombrado. En fin, creo que es uno de los lugares que me he quedado mas que asombrado.. No se lo pierdan...

text_clean: Machu Picchu ⛰ La forma más barata de visitar Machu Picchu es teniendo tiempo: ⛰ De Cusco tomar un transfer hasta Ollantaytambo temprano en la mañana y el trayecto dura 1 hora con 30 minutos. La agencia te recoge en el hospedaje. ⛰ Luego otro viaje en tren local y como es tan solicitado por los propios Peruanos, toca hacer la reserva con tiempo para poder encontrar cupos; la prioridad es para los locales. ⛰ Para subir a las ruinas, el paquete incluye la entrada pero si llegas por ti mismo; existen dos formas: la paga y la gratis. Puedes usar el bus que vale para extranjeros 12 dólares por trayecto. Aceptan efectivo y tarjeta. El viaje tarda 25 minutos. ⛰ La forma gratis es subir toda la montaña en un sendero señalizado y la subida dura casi tres horas. La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.
label: positivo

text_clean: 😉Fue un deseo de visitar a la Montaña Huaynapicchu y ver parte de las ruinas del gran imperio inca, como dicen por ahí la montaña es un reto mas aun cuando esta en épocas de lluvia el camino es difícil y muy peligroso, pero no me importo por que escalaba esa montaña en el día de mi cumpleaños; tuve una gran suerte de que mi agente turística estaba muy pendiente de mi y se las recomendare busque a la señora Linda Illatupa su Whatsapp es 939177232, hagan presente mi nombre para que reciban una rebaja en sus tours los demás que pululan por la plaza te incrementan cuando no conoces los precios. Lo único malo y no es por parte de los agentes turísticos es el problema del tren mi agente en todo momento me decía que ni bien baje del bus de retorno inmediatamente reservar tu boleto del tren si no están pendientes uds. mismo pueden perder el tren y quedarse un día en el pueblo y el hostal mas barato esta 120 soles y la comida ni que decir, el tren algunas veces llega a llevar solo 400 o 500 pasajeros y eso si sacas con tiempo tu ticket de retorno solo hay 3 salidas del tren 2 5 y 9 de la noche, en ese dia que viaje era el numero 160 y mi retorno estaba programado a las 5:00pm y solo dijeron que del 30 al 200 subirán al tren y el resto turno de las 9:00pm de la noche ese mismo día a promediar 9:50pm cae una fuerte lluvia con crecida de rio y hubo derrumbe en esa zona hubo 3 desaparecidos y las demás personas quedaron varados todo esto me entere en la mañana con mi agente de turismo pues el decía que si abordaría en el tren y otras personas que estaban con ella mucha no llegaron en ese día....pero como dice lo vivido nadie me lo quita nada mas hermoso de conocer dicha maravilla👍

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.
label: neutro

text_clean: De los mejores lugares en el mundo, esta maravilla del mundo moderno es hermosa, está un poci alejada de la civilización y es caro llegar y entrar en ella, sin embargo lo vale. text_clean: Increíble lugar, aunque sea una vez en la vida debemos visitar! Para visitar MP, sacar las entradas con varios días de anticipación, ahora se hace todo desde la web de Machu Picchu, se puede pagar con tarjeta o en las oficinas que están cerca de la plaza Regocijo. Atención con el horario, porque solo se puede entrar en el horario especifico. Aun así, si te toca el último turno y no te conviene, puedes entrar hasta las 7:25 am siempre y cuando vayas con un guía. El guía cobra 20 soles por persona o 100 por un grupo familiar. Ya estando allí, habrá varios guardianes que verán que no se saquen el tapabocas en ningún momento. Lo niños tienen más tolerancia en ese aspecto. Está todo muy bien cuidado y señalizado. El detalle que malogra todo el paseo es el tren local, que como turistas nacionales tenemos la opción de tomar . Sin embargo, la tensión por conseguir un boleto es terrible, no debería ser así ya que al final todos viajan, la caseta de serenazgo te da un turno y con eso deberías evitar colas. Hay mucha especulación y también eso da pie para que algunos taxistas te quieran llevar a otras estaciones tratando de sacar ventaja e incluso engañar a los turistas. Tengan cuidado con eso. Fuera de eso, todo excelente 👍

text_clean: Sin duda puedes disfrutarlo más de noche. Eh visto algunos extranjeros, pero también eh visto a niños perdidos, a gente en pésimo estado, hay mucho perdidos, hace MUCHA FALTA la seguridad. Esa noche de las fotos que fui, un domingo por la noche con mi familia , vi a un hombre atacando a dos mujeres con gas pimienta. Una de ella lloraba y pedía ayuda a gritos y la otra la ayudaba mientras que grababa al atacante. A unos metros de mi en pleno show histórico de las aguas de las 7pm. Este atacó a la señora eh intentó huir pero solo había uno que otro intentándolo detener y grabándolo. Además justo antes de pegar mis entradas vi a una mujer atacar a otra mujer en plena entrada con mucho niños cerca, al parecer esta le quería robar la cartera. -Deben tener mucho cuidado si van.
